critique.summary: The article discusses a video by Corridor Crew that claims to have solved a longstanding problem in visual effects related to green screens. It highlights various challenges and proposed solutions in the realm of VFX.
mainPoints: The video presents a solution to the transparency problem associated with green screens.
It discusses how neural networks and procedural generation may help in solving VFX challenges.
critique.truthfulness: The claims made in the video are based on the creators' experiences and insights into VFX technology. However, without external verification or citations from recognized sources, the accuracy of the specific claims remains uncertain.
considerations: The article does not mention the potential limitations or criticisms of the proposed solutions.
There is no discussion on the broader implications of these advancements in the VFX industry.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the technical aspects and implications of a recent video on VFX techniques, particularly focusing on the use of synthetic data and the challenges posed by proprietary technologies.

comments.author: Springtime
comments.text: In an earlier video they made a couple years back about Disney's sodium vapor technique Paul Debevec suggested he was considering creating a dataset using a similar premise: filming enough perfectly masked references to be able to train models to achieve better keying. So it was interesting seeing Corridor tackle this by instead using synthetic data.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a relevant reference to previous discussions on VFX techniques, showcasing an understanding of the topic.

comments.author: diacritical
comments.text:
From ~04:10 till 05:00 they talk about sodium-vapor lights and how Disney has the exclusive rights to use it. From what I read the knowledge on how to make them is a trade secret, so it's not patented. Seems weird that it would be hard to recreate something from the 1950's.

I also wonder how many hours were wasted by people who had to use inferior technology because Disney kept it secret. Cutting out animals and objects from the background 1 frame at a time seems so mindnumbingly boring.
comments.analysis: The comment raises valid concerns about proprietary technology and its impact on the industry, though it lacks specific sources.

comments.author: ralusek
comments.text: I'm a software engineer that, like the vast majority of you, uses AI/agents in my workflow every day. That being said, I have to admit that it feels a little weird to hear someone who does not write code say that they built something, without even mentioning that they had an agent build it (unless I missed that).
comments.analysis: This comment critiques the representation of technical contributions in the video, prompting a discussion about the role of AI in creative processes.

comments.author: superjan
comments.text: Watched this a few days ago. The video is light on technical details, except maybe that they used CGI to generate training data.
comments.analysis: While the comment points out a lack of detail, it does not provide additional insights or suggestions for improvement.

comments.author: IshKebab
comments.text:
Pretty impressive results! Seems like someone has even made a GUI for it: https://github.com/edenaion/EZ-CorridorKey

Still Python unfortunately.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses enthusiasm but lacks depth in analysis or critique of the video content.

comments.author: vsviridov
comments.text: The community has managed to drastically lower hardware requirements, but so far I think only Nvidia cards are supported, so as an AMD owner I'm still missing out :(
comments.analysis: The comment provides a relevant observation about hardware requirements but does not delve into the implications or context.

comments.author: comex
comments.text:
See also this video comparing Corridor Key to traditional keyers:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=abNygtFqYR8
comments.analysis: While sharing a related resource, the comment lacks original insight or analysis regarding the main topic.

comments.author: Computer0
comments.text: Looking forward to trying it out, 8gb of vram or unified memory required!
comments.analysis: This comment expresses anticipation but does not contribute to the discussion on the video's content or implications.
